demos.title: Getting started with Typenote
demos.description: Create your first course and document, and take your first note.

steps.label: Welcome to Typenote — the notebook built for university courses. This is your dashboard: your courses on top, recent documents below.
Everything in Typenote lives inside a course. Let's create one.
Give it a name.
Pick a color — it tags the course everywhere in the app.
Here it is. Click in — every course gets its own page for documents and materials.
Now the first document. Click New Document.
Name it after the lecture.
And choose a page style — blank, lined, grid, or dotted. Lined feels like a real notebook.
And you're in. Just start typing.
Notice the corner — Typenote saves automatically as you write. No save button, ever.
That's the foundation: courses, documents, and notes — organized from day one.

demos.title: Taking notes in Typenote
demos.description: Type, handwrite, sketch shapes, paste images and zoom — all on one page.

steps.label: A Typenote page mixes typed text, handwriting, and images. Let's open today's lecture notes.
Start in Type mode — a full rich-text editor.
Formatting works like you'd expect — control B for bold.
Now switch to Draw, and the same page becomes paper.
Handwrite anywhere — underline, annotate, sketch.
Draw a rough circle and hold still for a beat — Typenote snaps it into a perfect shape.
Got a diagram from the slides? Just paste it. The image lands on the page, ready to move and resize.
Drag it under your notes.
And when the details matter, zoom — control and scroll, or pinch on a tablet.
Typed text, ink, shapes, and images — one page, saved automatically.

demos.title: Writing math in Typenote
demos.description: Describe math in plain English — Typenote writes the LaTeX, renders it, and lets you edit or copy it.

steps.label: Math is first-class in Typenote. Let's open a problem set and write some.
Start typing your working like in any note.
When you need an equation, type a colon, then an open curly brace — and a math box appears.
Now describe the math in plain words — and Typenote writes the LaTeX for you.
There it is — a properly rendered equation, sitting right in your notes.
Need fine control? Click the equation, hit Edit — power users can edit the LaTeX directly.
Copy gives you the LaTeX to paste anywhere — and pasted back into Typenote, it stays an editable equation.
Describe it in plain words, get real math — saved automatically with the rest of your notes.

demos.title: Import from Moodle
demos.description: Connect your university's Moodle and pull course materials into Typenote with one sync.

steps.label: Here's your dashboard. Your notes live in Typenote — but the lecture files you need are still sitting on Moodle.
Lecture slides, problem sets, past exams — Typenote can pull them all straight into your workspace.
First, tell Typenote where your Moodle lives. Paste your university's address and hit Connect — Typenote remembers it from now on.
Connected. Now one click scans Moodle for everything you're enrolled in.
There they are — every course from my Moodle account. I only want Linear Algebra for now, so I'll leave the other one out.
Next, Typenote peeks inside the course and lists every file it can import.
Everything is organized by week, just like on Moodle — lectures and problem sets, already selected. Untick anything you don't need, and sync the rest.
Hit sync, and Typenote downloads each file from Moodle and stores it with your course.
It saves the course structure first, then pulls the files down one by one.

And that's it — your Moodle course, inside Typenote, ready to read and annotate. When new files appear next week, one click re-syncs just what's new. No more hunting through Moodle tabs the night before an exam — it's all in one place.

demos.title: Ask the AI about your course
demos.description: Chat with an AI tutor that has read your course materials — with citations.

steps.label: Every course in Typenote comes with its own AI tutor — one that has actually read your course files. Let's open Linear Algebra.
The chat lives in the corner of every course page. Open it up.
The free plan includes fifty chat questions a month, and your remaining balance is always visible — right here, above the input.
Now just ask, in plain language — exactly like you'd ask a teaching assistant after class.
The answer arrives right beside your course — a definition, the steps to follow, and clean math notation you can actually read.
And it's grounded in your own course materials — this citation points straight to Lecture 3, pages four to six.
Every conversation is saved with the course, so you can pick up any thread again before the exam.
And when you move on to a new topic, a fresh thread is one click away.
Your notes, your files, and a tutor that's read them all — that's the Typenote AI.

demos.title: Bring in your own files
demos.description: Import lecture PDFs into a course and read them side by side with your notes.

steps.label: Slides and PDFs belong right next to your notes. Let's bring one into Linear Algebra.
Click Import File and pick any PDF or Word document.
It lands under Materials — and the AI has already read it.
Now open your lecture notes, and click Focus files.
Add the file you just imported.
Click it — the PDF opens right beside your notes, and the AI focuses on exactly these files.
Scroll, and zoom in when the details matter.
Your files, your notes, and the AI — all on one screen.

demos.title: Export, share & version history
demos.description: Turn a document into a PDF, roll back to an earlier version, and share a course with a read-only link.

steps.label: Typenote doesn't lock your notes in. Let's export a PDF, roll back a document, and share a course.
Here's the midterm summary.
Need a copy to print or upload? One click turns the page into a PDF — math, ink and all.
Now the time machine: Typenote snapshots your work automatically.
Let's open the earliest snapshot — from before the summary was finished.
Restoring is safe — Typenote snapshots the current state first, so nothing is ever lost.
We're back in time — and the newest work is kept right there as a Before-restore snapshot.
Last one: sharing. The course tag takes us straight to the course page.
Click Share — viewers get the course materials read-only; your own notes stay private.
Create a viewer link — one URL your whole study group can use.
Copy it and send it. Contributor links work the same way.
Export it, roll it back, share it — your notes go wherever you need them.

demos.title: Organize with courses & folders
demos.description: Course pages, color tags, folders, and moving notes — keep a whole degree tidy.

steps.label: Courses are how Typenote organizes your degree — one card per course, each tagged with its own color.
That color follows the course everywhere — on its tags, its links, and its dialogs.
Let's step inside Linear Algebra.
Everything for the course lives on this one page. Your notes sit in the Documents grid.
Below them, Materials — the slides and files you import. The course A.I. reads these when you ask it questions.
And up top — create a new document, import a file, or share the whole course with your study group.
The Dashboard button takes us back.
As the semesters pile up, folders keep things tidy. Let's make one.
Call it Semester B, give it a color, and create it.
Notes can move right in. The Calculus lecture has a Move option in its menu.
Pick Semester B — and move it here.
Open the folder — and there it is, filed away.
Courses, colors, folders — your dashboard stays clean as your degree grows.

demos.title: Share a course with your study group
demos.description: Create a share link as the course owner, then follow it as a classmate — sign in, auto-join, and see the shared course from the viewer's side.

steps.label: Notes are better together. Let's share a course with the study group — and then see exactly what a classmate gets.
Maya wants her group inside this Linear Algebra course, materials and all.
Every course has a Share button — and it offers two kinds of links: viewer and contributor.
Viewer first. Viewers can read everything in the course — and edit nothing.
There's the link. Copy it once and drop it in the group chat.
And when you want classmates adding their own files and summaries, that's the contributor link.
Now let's switch seats and be the classmate. Daniel opens the link from the group chat.
He isn't signed in, so Typenote asks him to log in first — the link remembers where he was headed.
One click — and he lands straight inside the shared course. No invites to accept, no setup.
Daniel can open every material Maya shared. Her own notes stay private — and a viewer can't add files or manage sharing.
Back on his dashboard, the course now sits under Shared with me — by Maya Levi.
One link — and your whole study group works from the same course. That's sharing in Typenote.
